metadata.title: Build a Brand Archetype Classifier with Claude
metadata.purpose: Show students in Nina Harris' Branding and AI course how to build a five-input archetype classifier that maps a brand to the Mark & Pearson 12-archetype system, surfaces the shadow risk, and generates downstream forcing functions.
metadata.audience: Students and practitioners in a university branding course with an AI component
sources: Branding and AI, Chapter 5 — Brand Archetypes as a System (Nina Harris)

beats.narration_text: Hej — this is Liam, in for Bear. For Nina Harris' course, Branding and AI, here is the question we are running today: without a committed archetype, every brand decision is an open debate. With one, every decision is a filter. We are building the classifier that makes the archetype chooseable in five inputs.

props.command: Mission: [MISSION]. Audience adjectives: [ADJ1, ADJ2, ADJ3]. Proud product decision: [DECISION]. Tone example: [HEADLINE]. Competitor: [COMPETITOR]. Identify primary archetype, shadow risk, forcing function, and drift signals.
output: Forcing function: warmth over urgency in all copy
Drift signal: pricing language in input 3

beats.narration_text: Here is what breaks without it. You pick a visual palette, write some headlines, run some ads. Then your growth team writes copy that sounds like a different brand. Your designer ships something that feels wrong. Your product manager approves a feature that contradicts the whole identity. Nobody is wrong — there was just never a constraint. The archetype is the constraint.

props.sparkLine: There was just never a constraint.

beats.narration_text: The theory from Chapter Five. Mark and Pearson's twelve-archetype system. Each archetype has a core motivation, a shadow risk baked in, and a set of downstream decisions it makes chooseable. The Innocent archetype's core motivation is to be good and find happiness. Its shadow is denial of anything that complicates that simplicity. The forcing function: every copy decision now asks, does this sound like warmth and openness, or does it import urgency and scarcity the archetype can't carry?

The centerpiece: this is the classifier's output for Tropicana's five inputs. Primary archetype: Innocent — the rationale grounds in the mission of everyday goodness and the nostalgic tone of the headline input. Shadow risk: denial — specifically the packaging redesign in 2009 that stripped the orange-and-straw recognition asset, the one thing the Innocent archetype must never do. Forcing function: three downstream decisions now decidable — copy stays warm and simple, visuals stay bright and organic, features prioritize accessibility over novelty. Drift signals: one input pointed toward the Creator archetype, flagging mixed identity. Source: Branding and AI, Nina Harris, Chapter Five.

Honesty about what the classifier does not do. The classification is not fixed or permanent. Archetypes can evolve with deliberate repositioning — the tool surfaces the current-state, not a permanent label. And the five inputs are only as good as the honesty of whoever supplies them. A mission statement written for the website, not for how the company actually makes decisions, will produce a flattering misclassification.

artifactLines: The archetype is a current-state diagnostic, not a permanent label.
Archetypes can evolve — deliberate repositioning is valid.
Inputs must be honest, not aspirational, or the drift signals are useless.
The classifier names the constraint; humans still have to keep it.

beats.narration_text: Let us recap with Claude. Brand Archetype Classifier. Four lines.

artifactLines: Five inputs → primary archetype, shadow, forcing function, drift signals.
The archetype is the constraint that makes every downstream decision binary.
The shadow risk is built in — it is not a bug; name it so you can watch for it.
Tropicana lost its recognition asset by drifting its Innocent archetype.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Take this prompt into your Claude session: You are a brand archetype analyst using the Mark and Pearson twelve-archetype system. Here are my five inputs: Mission equals blank. Audience adjectives are blank, blank, blank. One product decision I am proud of is blank. My tone example is blank. My primary competitor is blank. Identify my primary archetype with a one-sentence rationale grounded in at least two of my inputs. Name the shadow risk and one brand that drifted into it. Give me three downstream decisions the archetype now makes decidable. Flag any of my five inputs that point toward a different archetype. Run this on your actual brand or your own personal brand as a student. The drift signals are the most valuable part — they show you where your identity is already inconsistent.

props.command: Brand archetype analysis using Mark & Pearson 12-archetype system. Mission: [MISSION]. Audience adjectives: [ADJ1, ADJ2, ADJ3]. Proud decision: [DECISION]. Tone: [HEADLINE]. Competitor: [COMPETITOR]. → Primary archetype + shadow + forcing function + drift signals.

beats.narration_text: Build a Brand Archetype Classifier with Claude. Liam, in for Bear.

props.title: Build a Brand Archetype Classifier with Claude
